summary: Joel shares results from an A/B test on FogBUGZ's demo signup page, where half of visitors were asked for an email address and half weren't. He found that simply requiring an email address caused about half of potential customers to abandon the signup process. Based on this, they removed the email requirement entirely. He connects this to a broader usability principle: even small reductions in friction can dramatically increase completion rates.
key_points: An A/B test showed that requiring just an email address cut demo signups by roughly 50%
Even minimal friction in signup flows causes massive user drop-off due to privacy and spam concerns
Reducing difficulty by even a small amount tends to double the number of people who succeed with a task
Joel also quotes Mark Newman dismissing CMM (Capability Maturity Model) as primarily a vehicle for consultants to sell books and seminars
money_quotes: About half of the people gave up when asked to type in an email address.
Reducing difficulty by even a small amount tends to double the number of people who succeed with a task.
We changed the demo signup so that 50% of the guinea pigs, er, potential customers had to provide an email address and 50% didn't.
Many of the free software demos you find on the web require a complete name, address, where did you hear about us, birthday, mother's driver's license ID, etc.
I view it primarily as a means for high-priced consultants to hold seminars and sell books, not as a process to improve software.
key_insight: Even the smallest friction in a user flow — like asking for an email address — can cut your conversion rate in half, so remove every unnecessary barrier.
